Use retry capabilities of adapters (HTTP, SuccessFactors) in addition to JMS, never alone. Adapter features are limited and cannot replace JMS richness.

Think of it this way: When connectivity issues arise, let the adapter try first (HTTP: max 3 retries, no backoff configuration). If insufficient (more retries needed, undefined HTTP status), JMS catches it - messages always end up in queue or DLQ. You don't want to see Failed status too often or too quickly.

 

Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) vs. JMS

Using JMS, we've built fully decoupled, resilient interfaces with guaranteed delivery. So why would we need Event Broker at all?

Does EDA still provide benefits? Do we still need an Event Bus like Event Mesh or AEM?

Yes, but for different reasons than you might think.

⚠️And let's be clear: "event-driven sounds impressive in architecture reviews" is not one of them. Neither is "it'll look good on my CV". Event Broker earns its place through actual technical advantages.

When JMS Excels: Point-to-Point with CI Orchestration

For point-to-point and async request-reply where a sender communicates with a single receiver, JMS is the ideal choice when CI already provides necessary mediation and orchestration beyond the basic routing and queueing capabilities of an event broker.

When Event Broker Excels: Pub-Sub Patterns

For Pub-Sub broadcasting through topics (Publish-Subscribe pattern: one sender, potentially many receivers), an Event Broker is always the correct choice.

Yes, you can technically replicate pub-sub in CI. You can successfully create a Recipient List pattern using dynamic routing to many receivers based on varied content (payload, headers/properties, HTTP path/query parameters...). For this, use Partner Directory and XSLTs while maintaining decoupled flows with independent queues per every receiver. Advantages: Partner Directory does not require redeployment, directly configurable on target tenant (no transport), therefore very flexible ... and dangerous tool.

Generally the question becomes: Better to force one tool (CI) to handle everything (doing pub-sub gymnastics) or use specialized tools for specific use cases (CI for mediation/orchestration, Event Broker for pub-sub)? If this isn't obvious, check ISA-M.

EDA's decisive advantage: True decoupling through dynamic subscription. Receivers add themselves to topics without touching the middleware. This flexibility - not just guaranteed delivery - is what separates event-driven architectures from traditional message-oriented middleware.

The Gray Zone: Event Broker for Point-to-Point?

Even for point-to-point, Event Broker can be the better choice when:

  • Enterprise EDA strategy mandates routing all async through Event Broker for consistency
  • No CI mediation needed (pure routing, minimal transformation)
  • JMS limitations become constraining
⚠️But stop cargo-culting Event Broker. 'It might become pub-sub!' is not architecture - it's hedging your bets with someone else's budget. Yes, requirements change. Yes, some point-to-point flows become pub-sub. But when only 1 in 10 integrations actually needs that second subscriber, deploying Event Broker for all 10 is waste. Design for today's reality, refactor for tomorrow's actuality. YAGNI applies to infrastructure too.

Cost Considerations

For high-throughput interfaces, Event Brokers are more cost-effective, as they are charged by data volume (GB) rather than by egress like Integration Suite (charged per outbound messages).
Note: Messages sent out through JMS receiver adapter are not considered as outbound messages because JMS adapter is internal adapter (like ProcessDirect) which makes usage of JMS "free of charge"

SAP S/4HANA Integration

For utilizing standard S/4HANA events, Event Mesh with its CI AMQP adapter is the best match.

Advanced EDA Requirements

For sophisticated EDA needs Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) is the answer

Example use cases:

  • Event streaming with replay capabilities (Kafka-like)
  • Request-reply with Reply-To destination for long running processes
  • Non-exclusive access type with partitioned queues for order ensurance and scalability
  • Direct point to point - direct messaging without persistent endpoint for performance over reliability
  • Persistent topic endpoint - as an alternative to queue endpoint, example use case: JMS durable subscribers
  • Very demanding high-volume integrations

 

JMS: The King of CI Workflow Async Processing

Central thesis: JMS is the king of point-to-point async processing within CI workflows. This single adapter - available freely in all CI editions (except Basic) - converts synchronous flows into fully decoupled, reliable processing when CI provides necessary mediation and/or orchestration.

This single adapter brings CI close to PI/PO in decoupling capabilities. While PI provides these qualities through configuration, CI achieves them through BPMN freestyle design - a respected successor with modern look and feel.

Stop treating SAP CI only as a web service mediator and orchestrator. With its built-in JMS message broker, CI supports high-throughput, reliable, asynchronous messaging

But recognize its domain: JMS excels at async processing within CI's orchestration scope. For pub-sub patterns and enterprise-wide event distribution, Event Broker wears the crown.

Know which kingdom you're in:

  • Point-to-point with CI mediation/orchestration: JMS is king
  • Pub-sub and enterprise EDA: Event Broker is king
  • Both exist in healthy integration landscapes

 

Final Thoughts

Many colleagues argue that synchronous interfaces are superior because the sender quickly learns operation results - nothing remains on the middleware side, clean hands for us. This is true if our goal is to minimize responsibility and simplify our lives short-term.

When considering what's best for the project and customer overall:

For point-to-point integrations with CI orchestration: Incorporate "ASYNC-first" and "JMS-first" alongside "API-first."

For business events and pub-sub patterns: Embrace "EDA-first" with Event Broker from day one.

These aren't competing philosophies - they're complementary patterns for different integration scenarios. Master both. Use each where it excels.

 

Share your thoughts

What are your experiences with JMS and asynchronous processing? I'd love to hear your stories and challenges in the comments below.

If I missed something important or you do not agree - feel free to comment as well, happy to discuss 🙂

entry.title: Announcement: Register to the new CEI project for SAP Integration Suite in 2026
entry.content:
Like in the previous years, we will run a Global Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) for SAP Integration Suite also in 2026.

Last year, we had 180+ registered customers and partners from over 20 countries worldwide. We ran multiple feedback sessions covering various capabilities and strategic investment topics of SAP Integration Suite such as "SAP Process Orchestration move" (ABAP proxy generation, mass migration, migration assessment, migration agent, etc.), AI & gen AI in the context of SAP Integration Suite in general and for B2B in particular, monitoring and operations, new adapters and adapter enhancements, reusable APIs and governance of API key access, event driven architecture, etc.

In 2026, we will continue collaborating with our customers and partners to gather feedback on product improvements, enhancements and new developments to validate the planned enhancements at an early stage, and ensuring that what we build will meet your business needs.

This will include open discussion about our current product backlog from the roadmap explorer as well as addressing pain points and topics that we collected in last year's CEI project such as more on Edge Integration Cell, AI, SAP PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite migration, Event Driven Architecture, ISA-M, best practices and guidelines, developer experience, etc. 

If you like to join the upcoming SAP Integration Suite influence project for providing feedback and shaping the SAP Integration Suite product, please register here (link leads to the customer influence page).

Registration is open from 9th of February 2026 until 13th of March 2026.

Once, the registration closes, we will invite you for an initial call where we go through the scope and the prerequisites of the feedback project. This will be around end of March/ begin of April so that we are able to start the project latest in April this year.

We will run monthly feedback sessions through the end of the year and hold a closing call to summarize our learnings, review the feedback collected, and discuss next steps.

Register today to ensure you do not miss this opportunity to influence and contribute to shaping SAP Integration Suite to meet your business needs. 

We look forward to your active participation and valuable feedback.

entry.title: Joule Is Not a Chatbot: How AI Agents on SAP BTP Help North American Utilities Reduce Risk
entry.content:
Joule Is Not a Chatbot: How AI Agents on SAP BTP Help North American Utilities Reduce Risk

 

Introduction: The Misunderstanding That Holds Utilities Back

Across North America, utilities are under pressure to modernize customer experience while reducing operational risk. When SAP introduced Joule, many utility teams assumed it was “just another chatbot.” It isn’t.

Joule is an AI agent framework that understands business context, triggers governed processes and interacts with SAP systems safely — something a traditional chatbot cannot do.

Utilities don’t need another conversational interface. They need intelligent agents that reduce risk, automate decisions, and protect the clean core.

This is where the SAP BTP AI Foundation becomes the strategic control plane, allowing utilities to innovate without modifying their S/4HANA or IS-U core—preserving a Clean Core while delivering cutting-edge automation.

⭐Why Joule Is More Than a Chatbot

During my learning journey with Joule, one misconception stood out immediately: many people assume Joule is “just another chatbot.” But when you look at how it works on SAP BTP, it becomes clear that Joule is fundamentally different.

A chatbot answers questions. A Joule Agent acts.

  1. Intent Detection: Unlike a chatbot that triggers keywords, Joule identifies a utility consumer's true intent (e.g., a "high bill" might actually be a "meter dispute").
  2. Policy Enforcement: Joule checks SAP BTP Business Rules before any transaction occurs.
  3. Action-Oriented: It initiates the SAP Build Process Automation workflow directly.
  4. Reliable (RAG): Joule leverages Retrieval-Augmented Generation via the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine to provide answers grounded in actual utility regulatory manuals, eliminating hallucinations.
  5. Responsible: Every action follows SAP BTP’s security framework, ensuring PII is masked and every interaction is auditable.

In regulated industries like utilities, this shift from conversational assistance to policy-driven automation is significant. It’s not about chatting — it’s about operational discipline.

⭐The Real Problem: Utilities Don’t Need Chatbots — They Need Governance

Most utilities already experimented with chatbots. The results were predictable:

  • Inconsistent answers
  • No integration with IS‑U
  • No audit trail
  • No process governance
  • High risk of incorrect customer commitments

A chatbot can talk. An AI agent can act — safely, consistently, and with full traceability.

That difference is everything.

⭐A Reference Architecture Use-Case

A large North American utility was struggling with high operational risk in its customer contact center. Agents were making inconsistent decisions about:

  • High‑bill disputes
  • Move‑in/move‑out exceptions
  • Meter investigation requests
  • Payment arrangement eligibility

The utility deployed Joule AI agents on SAP BTP, connected to IS‑U through Integration Suite.

Based on scenario modelling and validated utility process patterns, this agent‑orchestrated design is expected to:

  1.  reduce manual exceptions by ~35%, and

  2.  improve billing accuracy by ~18%. These are directional estimates, not production KPIs.

This is the difference between a chatbot and an AI agent.

 

⭐Architecture: How AI Agents Actually Work in a Utility Landscape

The architecture relies on the SAP BTP AI Foundation. Joule acts as the orchestration layer, while SAP Integration Suite serves as the governed gateway to the IS-U backend. By utilizing SAP Build Process Automation, we ensure that the AI agent never executes a 'Write' command directly to the ERP; instead, it triggers a governed workflow that includes Human-in-the-Loop approval for high-risk billing adjustments.

⭐How It Works: The 5‑Step AI Agent Flow

1.      Intent Identification: Joule receives a request and identifies the underlying business need.

2.       Policy Check: The agent checks eligibility and risk flags against business rules.

3.      Workflow Trigger: SAP BTP initiates a governed workflow via SAP Build.

4.      Human-in-the-Loop Validation: High-value billing exceptions are routed to a supervisor’s 'My Inbox' for final sign-off.

5.      Clean Core Integration: Integration Suite updates IS-U (Billing, FICA, or Device Management) via OData APIs, keeping the core clean.

This is what separates a safe enterprise AI agent from a generic chatbot.

⭐Why This Matters for Utilities

Utilities operate in a world where:

  • Every customer interaction is regulated
  • Every billing decision must be auditable
  • Every exception has financial impact
  • Every process must protect the clean core

AI agents on SAP BTP give utilities:

  • Governed automation
  • Consistent decisions
  • Reduced operational risk
  • Faster customer resolution
  • A clean core that stays clean

This is not hype — it’s a practical modernization path.

⭐Key Takeaways

  • Governed Framework: Joule is an agent, not a chatbot.
  • SAP BTP is the control center, ensuring safe, auditassssble and compliant actions.
  • Utilities reduce risk by routing all decisions through workflows and rules.
  • Integration Suite protects the clean core by using OData/API-led connectivity instead of legacy 'Z' enhancements.
  • AI agents deliver measurable impact: fewer exceptions, faster resolution, higher accuracy.
  • Rapid ROI: Leveraging pre-built SAP Discovery Center missions for Joule allows utilities to move from pilot to production in under 90 days.

⭐Conclusion: The Future of Utility Modernization Is Agent‑Driven

Utilities don’t need conversational gimmicks. They need AI agents that understand processes, enforce rules, and reduce risk.

Joule + SAP BTP delivers exactly that.

This is the next evolution of utility modernization — and it’s already happening across North America.

entry.title: Unlock S/4HANA Cloud Secrets: Hybrid Events+APIs Strategy for Seamless BTP Data Flows
entry.content:
Strategic Financial Data Extraction from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to SAP BTP

Using Transfer Pricing as a Real-World Example

Transfer pricing represents a perfect use case to demonstrate generic financial data extraction strategies from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Multinational organisations need GL, Controlling, Material Ledger, and Asset Accounting postings to flow reliably to external engines hosted on SAP BTP—but Public Cloud’s “no direct database access” constraint demands architecturally sound patterns.

Why Transfer Pricing Perfectly Illustrates the Challenge

The business need: Calculate inter-company markups (cost-plus, resale-minus) using real-time ACDOCA postings across multiple currencies, profit centres, and cost elements.
The constraint: Pure cloud extensibility—no RFCs, no custom ABAP, no direct table extracts. 
The solution: Three proven patterns that work for any financial data integration scenario.

Pattern 1: Event Notifications (Real-Time Push Model)

How transfer pricing uses it: GL postings trigger automatic business event notifications routed through SAP Event Mesh to BTP applications.

 

Event Notification

Generic applicability: Works for any posting-driven process—revenue recognition, inter-company reconciliation, compliance monitoring. 

High-level activation:
  1. Integration configuration apps → Activate accounting-related event notifications
  2. Communication scenarios → Configure outbound event destinations
  3. Event Mesh subscription → Point to BTP service endpoint
  4. Payload contains: Company codes, ledger amounts (local/group), profit centres, material valuations

Pattern 2: Standard Query Services (Scheduled Pull Model)

How transfer pricing uses it: BTP service queries S/4HANA nightly for new/changed financial postings.

 

Query ServiceKey services (release-dependent availability):

  • Financial line item services → GL/CO/ML/AA postings
  • Profitability services → Margin analysis data
  • Material valuation services → Cost component details

Generic applicability: Perfect for scheduled reconciliation, historical backfills, or validation runs.

Pattern 3: Hybrid Extraction (Production Resilience)

Transfer pricing demands 99.9% coverage. Smart architects layer all three patterns:

Use CasePrimary PatternTransfer Pricing ExampleGeneric Use
Real-time GL postingsEvent notificationsInvoice → immediate markupAny posting trigger
Historical catch-upStandard APIsMonth-end reconciliationData migration
Day 1 implementationBulk extractionFull ACDOCA baselineInitial loads
Custom calculationsBTP processingException overridesBusiness rules

 Three-tier reference flow:

Hybrid ExtractionBTP Implementation Patterns (Transfer Pricing Example)

Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP)
service FinancialIntegration {
  entity Postings as projection on external financial services;
  entity PricingResults as projection on HANA staging;
}

 Pattern: Event handlers + scheduled queries → unified HANA staging → business rules.

ABAP RESTful Application Programming (RAP)
define root view entity FinancialDataView
  as select from standard financial services

Pattern: Analytical CDS views over external data → transactional services.

Architect’s Production Checklist

✅ Tenant validation: Test events/APIs in your specific S/4HANA Cloud environment
✅ Quota management: Monitor Event Mesh throughput + API rate limits
✅ Fallback design: API polling validates event stream completeness
✅ Quarterly readiness: SAP evolves event coverage and API fields continuously
✅ Pure cloud: Zero custom code in production S/4HANA

The Universal Pattern

Transfer pricing proves the strategy works for complex financial scenarios. The same architecture applies to:
  • Inter-company reconciliation
  • Revenue recognition automation
  • Compliance reporting engines
  • Analytics data pipelines
  • Any standard data object/model driven process

Universal PatternKey takeaway: This isn’t “just for transfer pricing”—it’s your reusable blueprint for any S/4HANA Cloud → BTP data integration.

entry.title: Publish events from CAP Node.js applications to Event Mesh of Integration Suite
entry.content:
In this blog I’ll show how to publish events from a CAP application to SAP Integration Suite’s Event Mesh. Both the CAP application and Event Mesh are located in the same BTP subaccount. I’ll skip the setup steps for Event Mesh and focus specifically on publishing events from the CAP application.

Step 1: start by creating a new CAP Node.js application in Business Application Studio:

cds init emis-samebtp
touch srv/service.cds
touch srv/service.js

Step 2: in service.cds, define a simple service with a function that triggers an event:

service GreetingService {
  function helloEvent(to: String) returns String;
}

In service.js, implement the function to emit an event to Event Mesh:

const eventTopic = 'cap/demo/v1/sample/hello-event';

module.exports = async (srv) => {
  const messaging = await cds.connect.to('messaging');

  srv.on('helloEvent', async (req) => {
    const target = req.data.to;
    const data = { To: target, Message: 'Hello world events!' };
    
    await messaging.emit(eventTopic, data);
    req.notify(`Event sent > ${eventTopic}`);
    
    return `Hello event: ${target}`;
  });
};

Step 3: add the necessary services for authentication and messaging:

cds add xsuaa --for-production
cds add enterprise-messaging-shared --for-production

Then update your package.json to configure the messaging service:

"cds": {
  "server": { "index": true },
  "requires": {
    "[production]": {
      "auth": "xsuaa",
      "messaging": {
        "kind": "enterprise-messaging-shared",
        "publishPrefix": "stc/emis/",
        "format": "cloudevents",
        "vcap": {
          "label": "event-mesh-message-client",
          "plan": "message-client",
          "name": "emis-samebtp-messaging"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

Step 4: generate the MTA deployment descriptor:

npm install
cds build --production
cds add mta

Edit the generated mta.yaml to ensure the resource definitions match your package.json configuration.

Step 5: run the build and deploy commands to push your application to BTP. 

cds build
cf deploy mta_archives/your-archive.mtar

Step 6: Testing the Integration

Navigate to the Event Mesh section in SAP Integration Suite. Select a queue and subscribe to the topic "stc/emis/cap/demo/v1/sample/hello-event". 

Then use the OAuth client credentials from XSUAA to call your CAP service "https://<your-hostname>:443/odata/v4/greeting/helloEvent?to=Mark".

 If everything is configured correctly, you’ll receive a response and see the event appear in your Event Mesh queue.

This setup is ideal for scenarios where you need real-time eventing between BTP applications and other SAP or non-SAP systems. Give it a try and let me know how it goes in the comments!

entry.title: Receive Events from Event Mesh of Integration Suite to CAP Node.js Applications
entry.content:
In a previous blog, we covered how to publish events from a CAP application to Event Mesh of Integration Suite. This blog post is the natural counterpart. Here, we'll walk through the steps to configure a CAP Node.js application to consume and process events from the Event Mesh of SAP Integration Suite (often referred to as EMIS). Both the CAP application and Event Mesh are in the same BTP subaccount, but it also works if they are provisioned in different BTP subaccounts.

This guide skips the initial Event Mesh provisioning and focuses on the application development, configuration, and deployment steps for event consumption.

Step 1: start by creating a new CAP Node.js application in Business Application Studio

cds init emis-consume
cd emis-consume
touch srv/consume-event.cds
touch srv/consume-event.js

 Step 2: Define the Service Handler in srv/consume-event.cds

service ConsumeEvent @(path: '/consume-event') {
    // Entity definitions are optional for a pure event consumer service
}

Step 3: Define the event handling logic in srv/consume-event.js

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {
    const messaging = await cds.connect.to('messaging');

    // Handle events in "$namespace/hello-world" topic
    await messaging.on('hello-world', (msg) => {
        console.log('> hello-world event received: ', msg.data);
    });

    // Handle events in "$namespace/greeting" topic
    await messaging.on('greeting', (msg) => {
        console.log('> greeting event received: ', msg.data);
    });
});

Step 4: add the necessary services for authentication and messaging:

cds add xsuaa --for production
cds add enterprise-messaging-shared --for production

Then update your package.json to configure the messaging service:

"cds": {
  "requires": {
    "production": {
      "auth": "xsuaa",
      "messaging": {
        "kind": "enterprise-messaging-shared",
        "publishPrefix": "$namespace/",
        "subscribePrefix": "$namespace/",
        "format": "cloudevents",
        "vcap": {
          "label": "user-provided",
          "name": "emis-consume-messaging-ups"
        },
        "queue": {
          "name": "$namespace/incoming"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

Step 5: Prepare the MTA Deployment Descriptor

npm install
cds build --production
cds add mta

You must then edit the generated mta.yaml file to ensure the resource definitions match the configuration in package.json. Specifically, verify that the emis-consume-messaging resource points to the correct user-provided service.

Step 6. Crucial Workaround: Mocking the Management Endpoint

During deployment, cloud foundry runtime attempts to create the queue defined in package.json via a management URI provided by Event Mesh. Currently, this can fail with the error: "Requested route does not match any of the defined routes."

Workaround: 

1). create event mesh service instance & key

  • service name: SAP Integration Suite, Event Mesh
  • service plan: message-client
  • name: emis-consume-messaging

With below parameters:

{
    "namespace": "stc/emis/consume",
    "options": {
        "management": false,
        "messaging": false,
        "messagingrest": false
    },
    "resources": {"units": 10},
    "rules": {
        "queueRules": {
            "publishFilter": ["${namespace}/*"],
            "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
        },
        "topicRules": {
            "publishFilter": ["${namespace}/*"],
            "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
        }
    }
}

Then create a service key and copy its value.

2).  Modify the service-key by replacing its management uri with a mock endpoint. The mockend endpoint should always return 201 when receiving PUT request, for example,

PUT https://stc-mock.free.beeceptor.com/hub/rest/api/v1/management/messaging/queues/testqueue

3). Create a User-Provided Service in your Cloud Foundry space using this modified service key (emis-consume-messaging-ups). This is the service your CAP application will bind to.

 

4). Login to Event Mesh of Integration Suite >> Configure, create the queue stc/emis/consume/incoming

5). add below topic subscription to the queue

  • stc/emis/consume/hello-world
  • stc/emis/consume/greeting

This configuration ensures any event published to these topics is delivered to your application's queue.

Step 7: Build and Deploy

mbt build
cf deploy mta_archives/emis-consume_1.0.0.mtar

Step 8: Testing

Go to the Test section of your Event Mesh in the Integration Suite. Send test events to the topics hello-world and greeting. 

In the BTP cloud foundry cockpit, navigate to your deployed application and check its logs.

You have successfully configured a CAP Node.js application to act as an event consumer from Event Mesh of SAP Integration Suite. Although we had to implement a workaround due to a current compatibility issue between CAP's queue management and Event Mesh's Management API, the overall pattern remains sound and functional. Hopefully, SAP will address this compatibility issue soon, making the developer journey even smoother.

entry.title: Migrating from ASANWEE to ASAAEMEE: From SAP Event Mesh to SAP Advanced Event Mesh
entry.content:
Introduction

Event-driven architectures are increasingly shaping modern SAP landscapes. Many customers started their journey using the NetWeaver add-on for event enablement (component ASANWEE) to publish business events from their SAP systems to SAP Event Mesh.

While this setup works well for basic SAP BTP scenarios, growing enterprise requirements — such as hybrid integration, advanced routing, and high availability — often require a more powerful event broker.

This is where the Event Add-On for ERP (ASAAEMEE) in combination with SAP Advanced Event Mesh becomes highly relevant.

This article applies to:

  • SAP ERP (ECC 6.0 and higher)

  • SAP S/4HANA (on-premise, private cloud, all versions)

and explains the technical migration steps from component ASANWEE to ASAAEMEE.

 

Prerequisits:

  • NetWeaver Add-On for Event Enablement is installed (component ASANWEE).
  • Event Add-on for ERP is installed (component ASAAEMEE, installed on top of ASANWEE).
  • SAP Advanced Event Mesh service instance is provisioned.
  • Service Key from the BTP is available.
  • Ensure that the erp_event_add_on entitlement is added to your subaccount in the SAP BTP cockpit.
  • The SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh instance must be of type standard plan, not default plan. For details on migrating to the standard service plan, refer to the SAP Advanced Event Mesh migration guide.

 

Migration Steps

Step 1: Create RFC-Destinations.

To establish the connection to the SAP Advanced Event Mesh, we will create three RFC-Destinations in transaction SM59.

1. Create RFC-Destination pointing to the REST endpoint of the Advanced Event Mesh (Available inside the AEM broker).

  • Type: G (HTTP Connection to External Server).

  • Target Host: AEM REST endpoint host.
  • Basic Authentication: Add credentials, listed in the AEM broker for the REST endpoint.
  • SSL: Active.
  • Connection test should return in a 405 Method Not Allowed.

      

   

2. Create RFC-Destination for the Handshake (Available inside the SAP BTP Service Key).

  • Type: G

  • Target Host: Handshake URL from the SAP BTP Service Key
  • Path Prefix: /handshake
  • SSL: Active.

3. Create RFC-Destination for the Token (Available inside the SAP BTP Service Key).

  • Type: G

  • Target Host: Token endpoint from Service Key
  • Path Prefix: /oauth/token
  • SSL: Active.

   

 

Step 2: Add Certificates to the Trust Store.

To enable secure communication with SAP Advanced Event Mesh, the server certificate must be trusted in your SAP system.

  • Open transaction STRUST.
  • Select the SSL Client PSE that is used in the RFC destination created in SM59 (usually SSL Client (Anonymous) unless a specific PSE was assigned).
  • Click Import Certificate.
  • Upload the certificate file (.cer / .crt).
  • Click Add to Certificate List and save.

 

Step 3: Enable the Event Add-On for ERP.

Inside your SAP Advanced Event Mesh, navigate to Event Add-On for ERP.

After enabling the service, the component can be installed on your SAP system.

 

 

Step 4: Activate BC-Set.

Business Configuration Sets contain customizing and configuration-related table entries required for proper communication with SAP Advanced Event Mesh.

Transaction: SCPR20

  • Enter BC-Set name:/ASADEV/ACI_BCSET_FRAMEWORK_AEM
  • Choose Activate.

 

Step 5: Copy Instance for SAP Event Mesh to new Instance for SAP Advanced Event Mesh.

  • Go to SPRO -> IMG -> Integration with Other SAP Components -> SAP NetWeaver AddOn for Event enablement -> Connection and Replication Object Customizing 
  • Or go directly to transaction /ASADEV/ACI_SETTINGS.
  • Copy the existing SAP Event Mesh instance to create a new SAP Advanced Event Mesh instance:

  • Replace RFC destinations with the ones created for AEM
  • Change Cloud Type from SAP_EM to SAP_AEM.

  • Hit Enter and copy all.
  • Skip errors for duplicate Event Linkages

  • Delete all Default Values (not required for SAP AEM):

  • Delete Error Type Mapping (not required for SAP AEM):

  • You can keep the message type in case you delete the old Instance pointing to the SAP Event Mesh.
  • Update message types if keeping the old SAP_EM instance::
    • Create new Message Type in transaction WE81.
    • Activate Change Pointer for the new message type in transaction BD50.

  • Add Event Linkages for the new message type:

 

  • Go to Header Attributes.
    • Delete all Header Attributes for SAP EM.
    • Add new Header Attributes for SAP AEM.

 

  • Go to Field Mapping and configure a dynamic topic.
    • Each level becomes a segment in the dynamic topic.
    • There are static segments (Default Values) and dynamic segments (Source structure and Source field).

 

Step 6: Add validation Instance for the SAP BTP.

  • Got to Connections and add New Entry.
  • A Validation Instance will be necessary for the authentication against the BTP.
  • Add RFC destination for Handshake (from Step 1).

 

  • Go to Default Values and specify:
    • ACI_CLIENT_ID: Client ID from the Service Key.
    • ACI_TOKEN_DESTINATION: RFC-Destination for the Token (from Step 1).
    • AUTH_TYPE: OAUTH (Certificate also possible; refer to official documentation).

  • Add client secret from the Service Key
    • Transaction: SPRO
    • Go to: Integration with Other SAP Components -> SAP NetWeaver AddOn for Event enablement -> Set the cloud connection password
    • Or go directly to transaction: /ASADEV/SCI_TPW
    • Select the Cloud Instance for validation: SAP_VALIDATION
    • Enter the client secret in the Cloud Shared Secret field and execute.

 

Step 7: Test the new connection to SAP Advanced Event Mesh.

After the steps from above we can now test our new connection to the SAP Advanced Event Mesh.

Change something in the Application, where your Event Add-on for ERP does have an Oubtound Object configured and check the monitor transaction /ASADEV/ACI_MONITOR.

Cloud response should be 200 OK.

On the left side of the Trace we can see the JSON and dynamic topic, that was used to send the message to the SAP Advances Event Mesh.

This was configured inside the Field Mapping of the Outbound Object.

Migration Completed

After these steps, your SAP system is successfully switched from SAP Event Mesh to SAP Advanced Event Mesh using ASAAEMEE.

  • No changes are required in event definitions.

  • Monitoring and validation ensure messages are delivered correctly.
  • Find more configuration options for component ASAAEMEE inside the SAP Discovery Center mission "Data Exchange to Connect SAP ERP with SAP Advanced Event Mesh for Event-Driven Integration".

entry.title: Discover and Publish Events from SAP Advanced Event Mesh in Developer Hub
entry.content:
Introduction

Event-driven architecture (EDA) is a cornerstone of modern integration, enabling systems to react in real time to business events with loose coupling and scalability. In this blog, we walk through how to connect SAP Advanced Event Mesh with SAP Developer Hub to discover and publish events, empowering integration teams and developers to explore and reuse event streams within the SAP ecosystem.This setup is especially relevant if you’re looking to build responsive integrations or microservices that react to business events across SAP solutions.

🟢 Delivered Roadmap Item

SAP has delivered manual discovery and publication of events from Advanced Event Mesh to SAP Developer Hub a...

For a complementary scenario focused on discovering events from SAP S/4HANA Cloud using SAP Developer Hub, see the blog “Discover S/4HANA Cloud Events in SAP Developer Hub.” That post walks through registering your S/4HAN...

 

1️⃣ Overview: Why Discover and Publish Events

  • Developer Hub acts as a central catalog for APIs and events.
  • Advanced Event Mesh is the cloud service that enables event distribution across hybrid landscapes.

Discovering events helps teams understand what events are available and reuse them consistently.

2️⃣ Set Up the Destination to Connect Event Mesh to Developer Hub

  1. Go to SAP BTP Cockpit → Subaccount
  2. Navigate to Connectivity → Destinations → Create from Scratch
  3. Fill in details:
    • Name, Type: HTTP
    • Base URL of Advanced Event Mesh
    • Authentication (e.g., BasicAuthentication with API token)
    • Custom property: sap.isuite.dh.aem.enabled = true

3️⃣ Register and Configure Your Advanced Event Mesh Instance

Describe how to ensure Event Mesh and its event APIs are set up for discovery.

  • Create event APIs or ensure existing ones are shared
  • Enable appropriate access permissions

Note: Only shared events will show up in Developer Hub

4️⃣ Discover Events in SAP Developer Hub

Steps to explore events once the destination is set:

  1. Open Developer Hub via Integration Suite
  2. Go to Admin Center → Manage → Content
  3. Select your Advanced Event Mesh business system

Browse events grouped by application domain

5️⃣ Create Products and Publish Events

Explain how to group events into products and publish them:

  1. In Developer Hub, select events you want to publish
  2. Create a Product (logical grouping of events)

Summary

In this blog you learned how to 

  • Connect Advanced Event Mesh with SAP Developer Hub
  • Discover and publish events for reuse
  • Enable developers to consume events in integration use cases
  • Publish the Product to make it discoverable by developers

entry.title: Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean Core, BTP Driven Strategy for ModernUtilities
entry.content:
Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean‑Core, BTP‑Driven Strategy for Modern Utilities

Executive Summary

If you’ve spent any time around utility billing operations, you know mass reversals aren’t rare — they’re part of the job. Sometimes it’s a rate issue, sometimes a meter read problem, and sometimes it’s just one of those unexpected data surprises that ripple across months of billing, these reversals often span multiple billing cycles and thousands of customers. While the operational process may take days to complete, the customer impact is immediate: as soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on their online portals. This triggers confusion, social media chatter, call‑center spikes, and reputational risk before corrected bills are ready.

What I want to share here is a practical way utilities can handle this without rewriting IS‑U or scrambling teams at 6 AM. We used SAP BTP to create a small visibility layer that sits between IS‑U and the customer portal — and honestly, it solved a problem that has bothered billing teams for years, event‑driven architecture, and lightweight AI to prevent premature credit visibility without modifying SAP IS‑U or customer‑facing systems. The solution introduces a “Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer” that controls what customers see during mass reversals, ensuring operational accuracy without customer panic.

The Practitioner Reality: When a Simple Reversal Becomes a Public Crisis

Every utility billing team has lived through this scenario:

  • A mass correction requires reversing 8–12 months of invoices.
  • The reversal job runs for hours or days.
  • As soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on the portal.
  • Screenshots appear on social media: “Utility X just gave me $900 credit!”
  • Call centers are overwhelmed.
  • Regulators ask questions.
  • The billing team is still in the middle of the correction.

The root cause is simple: SAP IS‑U posts reversals immediately, and customer portals read them immediately. And here’s the part nobody talks about: IS‑U has not built‑in buffer. The moment a reversal posts, the customer sees it. There’s no ‘hold this until we’re done’ button. That’s where the trouble starts. Utilities cannot legally notify customers in advance, and they cannot delay the operational correction. This creates a structural gap between internal billing processes and external customer visibility.

Why This Happens: A Technical Gap in Traditional IS‑U

SAP IS‑U was designed with a real‑time posting model:

  • Reversal document posts →
  • FICA updates →
  • Portal reads →
  • Customer sees credit

There is no concept of:

  • “Temporary suppression”
  • “Billing in progress”
  • “Visibility hold”
  • “Batch publish”

This is not a failure of IS‑U — it’s simply a design that predates modern digital customer expectations.

The Modern Solution: A Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP

Instead of modifying IS‑U or customer portals, utilities can introduce a lightweight, clean‑core extension on SAP BTP that controls visibility, not billing.

This layer sits between:

SAP IS‑U → BTP → Customer Portal / CIS / Mobile App

It does not change billing logic. It does not alter FICA. It does not modify the portal. It simply orchestrates when billing information becomes visible.

Key Capabilities

  • Detect mass reversals in real time
  • Flag accounts as “under correction”
  • Temporarily suppress credit visibility
  • Replace credit display with a neutral message
  • Release final billing once corrections are complete

This simple layer kept customers from seeing half‑baked credits and gave the billing team the breathing room they needed to finish the job properly.

Textual Representation

 

How It Works Using BTP Services

1. SAP Event Mesh — Detect Reversals in Real Time

Event Mesh listens for:

  • Billing document reversals
  • FKK document reversals
  • Mass job triggers

When reversal volume exceeds a threshold (e.g., >500 accounts), it triggers the suppression workflow.

2. SAP BTP Workflow — Manage the Correction Window

Workflow orchestrates:

  • Start of reversal window
  • Suppression period
  • Completion of rebilling
  • Release of final invoice

This creates a controlled, auditable process.

3. SAP BTP Business Rules — Define Visibility Logic

Rules can be configured without coding:

  • “If reversal count > X, suppress visibility for Y hours.”
  • “If account is in correction, show neutral message.”
  • “If rebill fails, extend suppression.”

This keeps the solution clean‑core and upgrade‑safe.

4. SAP HANA Cloud — Temporary Data Store

Stores:

  • Accounts under correction
  • Suppression flags
  • Workflow status
  • Rebill completion indicators

No changes to IS‑U tables.

5. SAP BTP AI — Predict and Prevent Customer Impact

AI can:

  • Identify accounts likely to call
  • Detect social media spikes
  • Predict rebill duration
  • Recommend suppression windows
  • Flag anomalies in reversal patterns

This turns a reactive process into a proactive one.

What the Customer Sees Instead

Instead of a large credit, the portal displays:

“Your billing is being updated. Your final balance will be available shortly.”

This avoids:

  • Panic
  • Misinterpretation
  • Social media escalation
  • Call center overload

Clean‑Core Alignment

This approach is fully clean‑core because:

  • No IS‑U modifications
  • No FICA changes
  • No portal rewrites
  • No custom Z‑tables in SAP
  • All logic runs on BTP
  • Fully upgrade‑safe
  • Works with ECC and S/4HANA

This is exactly what SAP means by “extensibility without modification.”

Practitioner Example

In one of the utilities I worked with, we had to reverse nearly 10 months of bills for over 180,000 customers. Within 45 minutes, screenshots of huge credits were all over social media. The call center was drowning. Regulators were pinging leadership. It was chaos — and all because the portal updated too early.

After implementing a BTP‑based visibility layer:

  • Mass reversals ran silently
  • No customer saw temporary credits
  • No social media chatter
  • No call center spike
  • Corrected bills were published cleanly

The billing team completed the same process with zero customer disruption.

Conclusion

Mass invoice reversals will always be part of utility operations. But customer visibility does not need to be immediate or uncontrolled. By introducing a Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP — powered by Event Mesh, Workflow, Business Rules, HANA Cloud, and AI — utilities can protect customer experience, reduce operational risk, and maintain clean‑core principles.

This is modernization where it matters: not in rewriting billing logic, but in orchestrating how and when customers see the results.

entry.title: Staging in SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) – An Introduction to "Config Push"
entry.content:
Introduction

Motivation and goal

In software development, it is standard procedure to use staging for developed content, running it through quality assurance (QAS) before pushing it to the production system (PROD). This avoids the risk of developing directly in the production environment.

This key functionality has been missing in the Advanced Event Mesh (AEM), leading to the problem that developing directly in the production system is dangerous, and manually copying every event from one AEM to another is tedious.

This problem is solved with the new "Config Push" functionality, which is the main topic of this blog post. 
In the next few minutes, you will get the long-awaited answer to the question:

"How can you effectively implement staging in the AEM?"

This blog seeks to enable you to fully utilize this new functionality and profit from a more robust and less tedious AEM architectural landscape that now natively supports staging.

Covered content

In the first chapter, a brief overview of the AEM and the Event Portal will be given to allow everyone to fully understand the new functionality. If you're already familiar with the AEM and the Event Portal, you can skip ahead to the second chapter, where a step-by-step tutorial on how to enable and use the functionality is provided. If you have limited time or just want to get a quick glimpse of the new functionality, you can also jump ahead to the last chapter, where the most important aspects and steps will be summarized.


Brief overview of the AEM and the Event Portal

AEM supports many key features that come along with the broker and the entitlement like the Event Portal, Micro-Integrations, Mission Control and Insights.
Overview of functionality
The key feature this blog focuses on is the Event Portal, a tool used for designing your events. To use it effectively, you should first consider your business processes that you have and then build events based on them. This design-first approach is developer friendly, as it allows you to create and refine all your events before pushing them to the system.
 
Designed Runtime in the Event Portal Designer
The Event Portal Designer also gives you a full holistic view of the ecosystem. Within the components view all the consumed applications and events are displayed and you are able to directly add subscribers.
Component view
It enables you to see which which applications publish and subscribe an event.
Event details
You can also view the details from an application's perspective.
Application Details
Now the question is, once you designed the runtime, how can you have a lifecycle version control in place and push all the created controls to the broker?
This question is answered by the new functionality "Config Push".
 

How to enable staging by using "Config Push"

Once you have a designed Application Domain, you can now push it to the running broker. 
All the available AEM brokers can be found under the "Cluster Manager" tab.
AEM Brokers in the cluster manager

 

Push applications to DEV broker

  1. First you have to have created a runtime and connect it to the broker
    1. Go to Runtime Manager
      1. Runtime manager 
    2. Click on "Create Modeled Event Mesh" at the top right
      1. Create modeled event mesh modal
    3. Click on the new Modeled Event Mesh 
      1. Open modeled Event Mesh
    4. Go to the "Event Broker Connections Tab"
      1. Event broker connections tab
    5. Click on "Connect Event Broker" and select the broker you want to connect to
      1. Connect event broker
    6. It should now look like this. Every push that will be done is now against this broker
      1. Event Broker Connections
    7. Under "Architecture" all the objects that will be pushed are displayed
      1. Architecture tab
  2. Next you have to add the Applications to the runtime.
    1. Go again into the Designer and click on your Application Domain
      1. Application Domain
    2. Click on the Application that you want to push and open the application details (can also be done via the components view)
    3. Add the runtime and broker in the configuration of the application 
      1. Configuration
      2. Select the broker that is also connected to the runtime
        1. Select broker
      3. Define the client credentials
        1. Set client credentials
        2. Client Credentials can be found in the Cluster Manager > Broker > Try Me
          1. Find client credentials
      4. Click "Save & Promote" Later
        1. Save and promote later
    4. Promote Application
      1. Go to "Promotions", click on "Promote Version" and select the broker
      2. Click on "Preview Promotion"
        1.  
      3. Click on "Promote"
    5. The pushed application should now have a checkmark. Now check that the application was deployed by navigating to the previously created runtime under Runtime Manager > Runtime > Architecture. The deployed application should now be displayed
  3. Congratulations. You now have pushed your first application to the dev environment. You can now redo steps 2.1 to 2.4 for the other applications that you want to push.

 

Push applications to the other environment brokers

To push your applications to other subaccounts, your landscape must contain multiple subaccounts with the AEM that have a one to one connection. One subaccount for development (DEV), one subaccount for quality assurance (QAS) and one subaccount for production (PROD). Note that this blog only demonstrates linking to the PROD environment.
 
With the "Config Push" feature, you can push your previously promoted applications to the other environments, such as QAS or PROD, directly from the DEV AEM Event Portal. This is possible even though the subaccounts are normally isolated and unaware of one another.
  1. First you have to set up the subaccount landscape by linking the secondary AEM subaccounts to the main DEV subaccount 
    1. PROD: Go to your PROD Subaccount and into Account Details > Account Settings
      1. PROD: Enable linking
      2. (DEV: When trying to enable it in the DEV subaccount it is disabled, as in the subaccount that is linked to another subaccount no designing should be taking place. All the designing should be done in the DEV subaccount. Once you enable the "Subaccount Linking" the Event Portal will be disabled)
        1.  DEV: Enable Linking
    2. PROD: Click on "Create Link Code"
      1. PROD: Create Link Code
    3. PROD: Click on "Copy"
      1. PROD: Copy
      2. DEV: Paste link code in the Event Portal subaccount
        1. DEV: Go to DEV subaccount AEM Environments Page
          1. DEV: Environment Page
        2. DEV: Create new Environment
          1. DEV: Create environment
        3. DEV: Select "For brokers in a secondary AEM subaccount
          1. DEV: select for secondary
        4. DEV: Name it accordingly and select "Production" if its the Production subaccount
          1. DEV: Select production
        5. DEV: Click on "Link Now", paste the "Link Code" that you copied previously and click on "Link"
          1. DEV: Paste and link
        6. DEV: Now you can create a "Modeled Event Mesh" by navigating to the Runtime Event Manager for the subaccount
          1. DEV: Create Modeled Event Mesh
        7. DEV: After that you only have to click on the Modeled Event Mesh, go to "Event Broker Connections", click on "Connect Event Broker" and connect the production broker
          1. DEV: Connect Event Broker
  2. Now you have set up the subaccount landscape, you can push your promoted applications and events to other stages!
 

Summary 

This blog introduced "Config Push," a new capability for the AEM designed to streamline configuration management across multiple environments. It provides a comprehensive overview of how to leverage the Event Portal to design and promote application configurations. The result is a more robust workflow for managing the AEM landscape.
 
The core process involves these four steps:
  1. Establish the Development Environment: Create a "Modeled Event Mesh" within the Runtime Manager and connect it to the designated development broker.
  2. Deploy to Development: Configure the target application within the Event Portal Designer, associate it with the development runtime, and use the "Promote" function to deploy it.
  3. Link Subsequent Environments: Generate a "Link Code" from a secondary subaccount (for example Production) and use it within the primary development subaccount to establish a trusted connection between the environments.
  4. Promote to Production: Once linked, create a new Modeled Event Mesh for the production stage, connect the corresponding broker, and promote the validated application configurations to the live environment.

I hope this introduction to "Config Push" has provided you some valuable and useful insights. 

Feel free to comment any questions and remarks!

entry.title: Clean Core approach with the ASAPIO Clean Core Check
entry.content:
Introduction 
In this blog, I am going to show how to use the ASAPIO Integration Add-on Clean Core Check as a proactive safeguard to ensure Clean Core compliance – without manually researching released objects, SAP Notes, or successor APIs. 

The idea behind the Clean Core Check is simple: whenever a payload or outbound object is defined, the framework validates whether the used tables or objects are released for usage in SAP S/4HANA (especially in cloud-ready or clean core scenarios). If not, it automatically suggests alternative released CDS views. 

In short: instead of discovering during an upgrade that a table like VBAK is not released, you detect it during design time – and receive a proposal such as I_SALESDOCUMENT as successor. 

This approach requires the ASAPIO Integration Add-on and is particularly relevant in S/4HANA environments where unreleased objects must be avoided. 

 

Why Clean Core matters 

SAP’s Clean Core strategy aims to: 

  • Reduce modification footprint 
  • Prevent direct access to non-released database tables 
  • Ensure upgrade stability 
  • Enforce usage of released APIs and CDS views 

Many classical SAP tables (e.g. VBAK, VBKD, VEDA) are technically available but not released for direct usage in S/4HANA Cloud or Clean Core compliant developments. 

Using them in custom extraction logic or event payloads creates technical debt and upgrade risk. 

This is exactly where the ASAPIO Clean Core Check comes into play. 

 

Example: Payload with classic SD tables 

Let’s assume we defined a Sales Order payload in the ASAPIO Payload Designer (transaction /ASADEV/DESIGN). 

We add the following tables: 

  • VBAK – Sales Document Header 
  • VBPA – Partner Data 
  • VEDA – Contract Data 
  • VBKD – Business Data 

The payload is configured and released. 

Now, instead of discovering issues during a system conversion or ATC run, we execute the Clean Core Check directly in the Payload Designer. 

 

Running the Clean Core Check 

Inside the Payload Designer, simply click on the Clean Core Check button in the toolbar. 

The framework evaluates all tables and objects used in the payload definition. 

 

 

What happens here? 

  • The system detects that VBAK, VBKD, and VEDA are not released objects 
  • It checks SAP’s released API model 
  • It proposes I_SALESDOCUMENT as an alternative CDS View 
  • It flags objects not listed in released content (e.g. VBPA) 

This happens instantly. 

 

Enabling the Clean Core Check  

Before the Clean Core Check can be executed, the corresponding repository configuration must be maintained. 

The check relies on the ASAPIO Clean Core repository, which contains the metadata about released and non-released SAP objects as well as successor CDS views. 

To activate and configure the check properly, follow the official documentation: 

https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1#use_clean_core_checks 

 

Refactoring to released CDS views 

After the Clean Core Check result, the next step is straightforward: 

  1. Remove the unreleased tables from the payload 
  1. Replace them with the suggested CDS view 
  1. Re-map the required fields 

In our example, instead of selecting from VBAK directly, we use CDS View I_SALESDOCUMENT. 

Benefits: 

  • Released API contract 
  • Semantic layer abstraction 
  • Upgrade stability 
  • Cloud readiness 
  • Compliance with Clean Core guidelines 

The payload can now be executed without violating SAP’s release strategy. 

 

Summary 

The ASAPIO Clean Core Check provides: 

  • Immediate detection of non-released tables 
  • Automatic suggestion of released CDS alternatives 
  • Support for SAP Clean Core strategy 
  • Reduced upgrade risks 
  • Faster development cycles 

Instead of reacting to ATC findings during system conversion, developers can proactively design compliant payloads from the beginning. 

It shifts Clean Core validation directly into the design phase. 

In other words: 

Configuration-driven integration, now also Clean Core compliant. 

Let me know if you would like to see a deeper technical walkthrough or how the check is implemented internally in the Add-on.

entry.title: Event Mesh + AMQP: The Retry Trap Nobody Warns You About
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I have seen a number of implementations of event-driven design involving SAP Event Mesh and the AMQP sender adapter on Cloud Integration. Most were built on the incorrect assumption that standard settings would be sufficient — without proper end-to-end testing to validate that assumption.

In this blog, I explain two patterns I have used in practice, along with their strengths and trade-offs. I have also added an important baseline section on why the native AMQP retry configuration alone is not enough.

Finally, we will look at whether Advanced Event Mesh requires the same workarounds — or whether it solves the problem out of the box.

Baseline: Why Native AMQP Retry Is Not Enough

The AMQP sender adapter does have a built-in Retry Details configuration (Max. Number of Retries + Delivery Status After Max. Retries). However, the native retry has a critical limitation: there is no configurable retry delay or backoff interval. The broker redelivers immediately and — if not set up correctly — indefinitely. This means a temporary downstream outage produces a burst of failures rather than graceful back-pressure.

Max Redelivery Count in Event Mesh queue configuration set as as EMPTY/0 or Max. Number of Retries in the AMQP sender adapter set as EMPTY results in indefinite, immediate redeliveries that flood both the EM queue and the CI tenant — a serious stability risk.

📝Remark: According to SAP Note 3269414, configuring a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) in Event Broker can help limit retries, but this is not affecting Event Mesh.

The practical hint: to effectively limit number of retries you need to set retry number > 0 in Event Mesh queue settings and AMQP adapter.

JMSXDeliveryCount header is auto-incremented only for internal AMQP retries, not overall number of retries (AMQP + Broker side). JMSRedelivered header switch from false to true. Therefore retry strategy cannot be designed inside Iflow similary to JMS (SAPJMSRetries header). A potential workaround is to use a custom header (please see the description in Option 2).

As you can see, we can limit the number of retries, but we can't set a solid retry interval. That's exactly why I wrote this blog post with two solutions.

Each pattern has its strengths and trade-offs. Let's dive in.

Option 1: Decouple AMQP from iFlow Logic via JMS

In my previous blog series on JMS, I compared whether to use JMS or an Event Broker directly:
JMS Mastery Series Part 3 — JMS in Production

Actually, both approaches can work in synergy for certain scenarios — retry handling being a prime example.

The core idea: use the AMQP adapter only to ingest the message from Event Mesh, immediately write it to a JMS queue, and let the JMS adapter handle all retry logic from that point forward. Event Mesh is decoupled from the processing logic as soon as the message lands in JMS. The iFlow finishes with COMPLETED status and the message is acknowledged and removed from the EM queue.

How it works

  • The AMQP sender adapter receives the message from Event Mesh.
  • The iFlow immediately writes the payload to a JMS queue, which acts as an internal buffer.
  • A second iFlow reads from JMS and executes the full processing logic.
  • JMS handles retries natively with a configurable interval and exponential backoff.

Pros

  • The Event Mesh connection is short-lived — released immediately after the JMS write, freeing EM resources.
  • Full JMS retry capability: configurable retry interval, exponential backoff, and maximum retry interval — none of which are available in the AMQP adapter.
  • Proven pattern — behaves identically to any JMS-backed HTTP or SOAP integration.

Cons

  • Consumes additional JMS resources — two queues are involved: the external EM queue and the internal JMS queue.
  • Increased operational overhead: more moving parts to monitor and maintain.

To reduce the number of JMS queues, you can assign one JMS queue per group of interfaces, or even one JMS queue for all interfaces from a single Event Mesh instance. However, this breaks the 1:1 mapping between EM queues and JMS queues, with important consequences.

💡Personal note: Even as a JMS advocate, I apply this pattern selectively. It is not the default. I add JMS on top of AMQP only when the iFlow logic is genuinely heavy — complex mappings, multi-step orchestration with high processing time or calls to multiple external systems (Recipient List pattern). Second use case is when you truly need robust retry control with exponential backoff. For operationally lightweight flows, Option 2 is cleaner.

Option 2: Retry Queue + TTL + DMQ (No JMS)

This pattern implements a retry-with-delay mechanism entirely within Event Mesh, using a dedicated Retry queue and TTL expiration to simulate retry backoff — without involving JMS at all.

How it works

  • The main iFlow catches errors in the exception subprocess.
  • If the error is transient and eligible for retry, the message is forwarded to a dedicated Retry queue in Event Mesh via an AMQP receiver adapter.
  • The Retry queue is configured with: DMQ = original processing queue, Max TTL = desired retry delay in seconds.
  • When the TTL expires, Event Mesh automatically moves the message to the DMQ — which is the original queue — and processing restarts.
  • The iFlow ends with an End Message event (COMPLETED status), so no automatic AMQP retry is triggered.

The TTL value effectively becomes your retry delay interval. You can implement increasing delays by chaining multiple retry queues with different TTL values, though this adds configuration complexity.

This creates a controlled retry loop: the message cycles between the Retry queue and the original queue until it either succeeds or the retry counter threshold is reached.

Tracking retry count

Because JMSXDeliveryCount resets to 1 and JMSRedelivered resets to false on every queue hop — this is a new delivery, not a broker redelivery — you cannot rely on these headers to count retry attempts.

Two approaches for maintaining a retry counter:

  • Custom header approach: add a RetryCount header to the message before forwarding it to the Retry queue, increment it on each pass, and stop retrying when it exceeds your threshold. This avoids any persistence layer and is the preferred approach for most scenarios.
  • Data Store approach: persist a counter keyed on a message correlation ID. This can introduce read/write lock contention at high throughput.

Pros

  • No JMS resources consumed.
  • Retry delay is cleanly controlled via TTL — no thread blocking.

Cons

  • The setup is non-obvious and requires careful EM queue configuration.
  • JMSXDeliveryCount and JMSRedelivered are unreliable for retry counting — custom counter logic is required.
  • A Data Store-based counter introduces contention risk at high throughput.
  • No native exponential backoff — fixed TTL only, unless you chain queues.

💡Personal note: This is my preferred option. Clean, JMS-free, and elegant once you understand the TTL mechanics.

Option 3 (Not Recommended): Groovy Sleep as Retry Delay

This is a workaround I have seen in the field: using a sleep() call inside a Groovy script to introduce a delay before retrying, relying on the AMQP adapter's built-in retry to re-execute the iFlow.

Pros

  • No additional infrastructure — no JMS, no Retry queue, no TTL configuration.
  • Simple to implement for a quick proof of concept.

Cons

  • The AMQP connection to Event Mesh remains open and locked for the entire sleep duration, preventing EM from reclaiming the resource.
  • The CI worker thread is blocked for the sleep duration. Under concurrent load, this exhausts the worker thread pool and degrades performance for all iFlows on the same tenant — not just this one.
  • sleep() in Groovy on Cloud Integration is a tenant-level anti-pattern. At scale, it can cause cascading timeouts across unrelated integrations. This is not merely an aesthetic concern — it is a production stability risk.
  • No retry limit control without additional explicit design.

💡Personal note: Even though it is simple to implement, never use this in production. It is a quick hack, but the connection lock makes it an anti-pattern for any scenario requiring scalability. I would not recommend it to anyone.

Pattern Comparison

Use this table to guide your pattern selection:

CriteriaOption 1: JMS BridgeOption 2: TTL + DMQOption 3: Groovy SleepAMQP Retry
JMS RequiredYesNoNoNo
Retry Delay Control✅Full — retry interval, exponential backoff, max retry interval✅Via TTL — retry interval only, no exponential backoff⚠️Via sleep command only❌Immediate redelivery only
Retry CounterSAPJMSRetries headerCustom header / Data StoreEM + AMQP settings or Custom header / Data StoreEM + AMQP settings or Custom header / Data Store
EM Connection DurationShort - decoupled from iFlow processingStandard - but prevents long-lived connections caused by retriesLong ⚠️Standard
High Throughput FitYes (with care)Limited if Data Store used as counterNoYes (with care)
Setup ComplexityMediumMediumLowLow
Recommended ForHeavy iFlow logic, strict retry needsMost event-driven cases — low-to-moderate iFlow logic, flat retry interval is sufficientNot recommended — development/POC use only at mostSimple flows only — needs redesign for production

Does Retrying in Advanced Event Mesh Work Out of the Box?

Short answer: yes — AEM retry logic works like a charm. This is not surprising given that Advanced Event Mesh is not an entirely new product, but rather a white-label of Solace PubSub+, a very mature messaging solution with years of production use behind it.

What Can We Expect from AEM and the AEM Sender Adapter in Cloud Integration?

The full spectrum of retry configuration is available directly at the adapter level:

  • Retry interval
  • Maximum retry interval
  • Exponential backoff multiplier
  • Maximum number of retries

All of the above are configured directly on the AEM adapter in Cloud Integration, and all work as expected. No surprises, no disappointments. It is the best of both worlds:

  • JMS-level retry interval configuration is available
  • Event Mesh-level retry limit configuration is available

AEM offers not only adapter-level retries (CI side) but also broker-level retries (Max Redelivery Count on the queue). After CI-side retries are exhausted, a failed status is sent back to AEM, which then redelivers the message according to its own Max Redelivery Count — so the total retry attempts is a product of both settings, not a simple sum. Factor this in when configuring both to avoid unexpectedly high total retry counts.
This works like described above if Acknowledgment mode is set to: Automatic On Exchange Completed and Settlement outcome: Failed.

If you are fortunate enough to be working with AEM on your project, you do not need to apply any of the workarounds described above or add JMS on top of your event-driven design purely for the sake of working retry logic.

Final Thoughts

These two patterns exist because the AMQP adapter's native retry has no configurable delay. Until SAP adds native retry backoff to the AMQP adapter — or Event Mesh provides broker-side delay queues as a first-class feature — these workarounds remain necessary for production-grade event-driven designs.

My default recommendations:

  • Start with Option 2 (TTL + DMQ) for most flows. It is clean, JMS-free, and operationally lightweight.
  • Escalate to Option 1 (JMS bridge) only when the iFlow processing logic is genuinely complex or you need exponential backoff.
  • Never use the Groovy Sleep approach in production.
  • If AEM is available, the standard adapter configuration gives you a complete, production-grade solution out of the box — no workarounds required.

Additional Notes for Production

Idempotency and duplicate handling: Complement your chosen retry pattern with idempotency handling to avoid duplicate processing in the target system. The best place to implement deduplication is the receiver application, not the middleware. Also note that the event broker does not guarantee exactly-once processing — you must design for duplicates and assume at-least-once delivery.

Define the final stop after retries: Always define what happens when retries are exhausted. Route the message to a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) or equivalent quarantine mechanism, and ensure there is an operational process for monitoring and reprocessing those messages.

Are you using any of these patterns? Have you found other approaches? Comments, questions, and observations are very welcome.
